entries.title: Organizer overview - a stash for interesting requests
entries.body:
# Burp Organizer - overview

**Organizer** is a workspace for **collecting and arranging HTTP messages** during testing. Its purpose is simple: set an interesting request (and its response) aside, attach a note and a status, and **come back to it later** without interrupting your current flow of work and without losing the find in the noise of the proxy history.

## Why you need it

During an active test, hundreds of requests fly past you. Something catches your eye - a suspicious parameter, a strange response, an IDOR candidate - but there is no time to dive in right now. If you just leave it in the **HTTP history**, ten minutes later the request will drown among thousands of others. **Organizer** solves this problem: you set the request aside in a separate "stash", and it will not go anywhere.

## Inbox and how messages get here

By default everything lands in the **inbox** - the starting collection of **Organizer**. You can send a message here from other Burp tools:

- right-click on one or several messages > **Send to Organizer**;
- or the default hotkey **Ctrl+O**.

Messages arrive in **Organizer** as **read-only copies** of the original request and response - together with all the notes and highlights that were already on them. This is important: in **Organizer** requests are **not edited** and not resent (that is what **Repeater** is for). Here they are **stored and annotated**.

## Table columns

The message table shows for each item: index (**#**), time, **working status** (Status), source tool (where it was sent from), HTTP method, host, path, query string, number of parameters, response code, response size in bytes, and **notes** (Notes).

## Viewing a message

Select a row - the request and response will open below **in view mode** (read-only). You can search within the message and switch between the **Inspector** and **Notes** tabs. There is nothing to edit here - the copy is immutable by design.

## How it differs from saving to Repeater or Site map

It might seem that you could set a request aside in **Repeater** (create a tab) or just find it later in the **Site map**. The difference is in purpose:

- **Repeater** is a workbench for **editing and resending**; keeping dozens of "saved for later" tabs there is inconvenient, and they easily get mixed up.
- **Site map** is a map of the **entire** application; your personal finds dissolve in it among everything that was captured.
- **Organizer** is a specialized **triage stash**: this is where you put exactly what you yourself flagged as interesting, and here there is structure (collections), statuses, and notes tailored for "come back and investigate further".

## Edition

**Organizer** is available in both **Professional** and **Community Edition**. The basic workflow - sending messages, inbox, notes, highlights, statuses, collections, and the filter - is present in both editions.

## Tip from practice

Make it a habit: **see something interesting - Ctrl+O**. Not "I'll remember it and come back" (you won't), but straight into **Organizer** with a short comment on **why** it is interesting ("parameter q is reflected", "looks like IDOR on id"). An hour later, when you finish the current branch, you will have a tidy list of finds with statuses instead of a vague feeling that "there was something there".

entries.title: Annotations in Organizer - notes, highlights, statuses
entries.body:
# Annotations in Organizer

The main value of **Organizer** is not just stacking up requests, but **giving them context**. For this there are three kinds of annotations: **notes (Notes)**, **highlights (highlight)**, and **status (Status)**. Together they turn the stash into a manageable triage list, where for each item it is clear what it is and what stage it is at.

## Notes

A note is your text comment on a message: what you noticed, what you suspect, what to check. To add a note, double-click the **Notes** column - the **Notes** panel will open, where you type the text.

An important detail: **notes and highlights travel together with the message** between Burp tools. That is, if you sent the request onward from **Organizer** (or, conversely, it arrived here already marked), your comments and color will not go anywhere - the documentation rides along with the item.

Examples of useful notes:

```
- "redirect parameter is not validated -> open redirect?"
- "id changes -> someone else's order is visible -> IDOR, check on a second account"
- "500 on a single quote in search -> SQLi candidate"
```

## Highlight

A highlight is a **color label** on a row for visual separation. Click the **#** column in the row and pick a color from the dropdown.

Color is handy as a quick code for severity or topic. For example:

- red - hot vulnerability candidates;
- yellow - "look at later", not sure;
- green - checked/confirmed;
- blue - a different category (for example, an infrastructure observation).

The main thing is to stick to **one** color system of your own per project, otherwise the highlighting loses its meaning.

## Status

Status is the **lifecycle stage** of a find, so you do not lose track of what has already been handled and what is still waiting. To set a status, click the **Status** column and pick a value from the dropdown. Available statuses:

- **New** - new, not touched yet;
- **In progress** - being worked on;
- **Postponed** - put off;
- **Done** - handled/closed;
- **Ignored** - ignored (false alarm, not interesting).

Statuses are what set **Organizer** apart from a plain bookmark list: they show the **progress** of triage, and they are also convenient to filter by (show, say, only **New** and **In progress**).

## How to apply an annotation to several items at once

Annotations can be set in bulk: select one or several messages, right-click, and choose the needed action from the menu. This is handy when you need, for example, to mark a group of requests with the **Done** status all at once or highlight them in one color.

## Practical scenario

```
1. In Organizer, new items have the status New by default.
2. You take an item into work -> Status = In progress, in Notes you write the hypothesis.
3. Confirmed the bug -> highlight red, add reproduction steps to Notes.
4. Turned out to be a false alarm -> Status = Ignored (but do not delete it, let it stay).
5. At the end of the session, filter to show only red / In progress
   -> this is your working list for finishing up and the report.
```

## Tip

Set up a **single** "color + status" pairing and stick to it across all projects: for example, red = confirmed bug, statuses run from **New** to **Done**. When the system is stable, a single glance at the **Organizer** table replaces reading all the notes - you immediately see where things are on fire and what is still not done.

entries.title: Filtering in Organizer - selecting the messages you need
entries.body:
# Filtering in Organizer

When dozens and hundreds of saved messages have accumulated in **Organizer**, you need a way to quickly select **only the ones relevant right now**. For this there is a **filter**. An important nuance: **the same filter applies to both the inbox and the collections** - switching between them, you see the data through the same selection conditions.

## Where it is and how to open it

Click the **filter bar** above the table - the **Organizer filter** window will open with all the criteria.

## What you can filter by

The set of criteria in the window:

**By request type:**

- show only **in-scope** items;
- show only items **with responses**;
- show only requests **with parameters**.

**By content:**

- **MIME type** - show/hide responses by type (HTML, CSS, images, etc.);
- **Status code** - by HTTP response code;
- **File extension** - by file extension (show/hide).

**By source:**

- **Tool origin** - show/hide items depending on **which** Burp **tool** they were sent to **Organizer** from.

**By annotations and status:**

- by **notes or highlights** (show only annotated ones);
- by item **status** (New / In progress / Postponed / Done / Ignored).

**By search:**

- **search term** in the content, with options: literal or **regular expression**, case sensitivity, **negative search** (show what did NOT match).

## Managing the filter

The window has buttons for managing the current set of conditions: **Show all**, **Hide all**, and **Revert changes**. Through the **settings icon** you can reset the filter to default values, and also **load** or **save** a filter configuration - handy for keeping ready presets for typical tasks.

## The filter is non-destructive

A key property: **the filter only controls display**. Items it hides are **not deleted** and will reappear as soon as you reset or change the conditions. So you can filter freely - nothing is lost, you are only changing the "viewing window" onto your stash.

## Practical scenarios

```
# Finishing finds up for the report:
Status = only In progress + highlight red
-> on screen only active confirmed bugs.

# Searching by topic:
search term "token" (regex off) + Tool origin = Proxy
-> all saved requests about tokens that came from the proxy.

# Cleaning up the noise:
MIME type: hide images/CSS, File extension: hide js/css
-> meaningful requests remain, not static assets.
```

## Tip

Remember that **the filter is shared** between the inbox and all collections. If you opened a collection and it is suddenly "empty" - first thing, check the **filter bar**: most likely an active filter is cutting off its content. Click **Show all** or reset the filter through the settings icon before deciding that messages have disappeared somewhere. And **save** good combinations of conditions as presets, so you do not have to assemble them again in every project.

entries.title: Collections in Organizer - grouping messages around your workflow
entries.body:
# Collections in Organizer

**Collections** are a way to **group** saved HTTP messages in whatever way is convenient for you and your testing process. If the **inbox** is the common "incoming pile", then collections are the folders into which you sort your finds: by vulnerability type, by application module, by feature, by priority - however you like.

## Why you need them

One common list quickly becomes unmanageable. Collections bring order: for example, a separate collection for **IDOR** candidates, a separate one for **SQLi** suspicions, a separate one for requests of a specific "Payments" section. This way at any moment you can see what is where, and it is easy to come back to the group you need.

## Creating a collection

Click the **New** button in **Organizer** - an empty collection will be created, ready to be filled. Give it a meaningful name that fits its purpose.

## Moving messages

To **move** messages to another collection:

1. Select one or several messages.
2. Right-click > **Move to...**.
3. Choose the destination collection.

Moving **removes** the message from its current place - it relocates entirely into the chosen collection.

## Copying messages

To **copy** messages (leaving the original in place):

1. Select the messages.
2. Right-click > **Copy to...**.
3. Choose the target collection.

Copying keeps the original and creates a duplicate elsewhere. This is useful when a request relates to **several** topics at once (for example, both to "authentication" and to "payment logic").

> You can move and copy not only between collections, but also **back to the inbox** - if you decide that a message does not belong to any group for now.

## Managing collections

Right-clicking the collection itself opens actions on it: **rename**, **duplicate**, and **delete**. In addition, a collection has an **About** tab where you can write a **collection-level** note - for example, describe what this group is and by what principle you put requests into it.

## Deleting a collection with messages

If you delete a collection that **has messages** in it, Burp will ask what to do with them: **return them back to the inbox** or **delete them for good** from **Organizer**. So you cannot accidentally lose finds when deleting a folder - you will be explicitly given the choice.

## How the inbox relates to collections

The **inbox** is the default place where messages land **before** sorting. The typical flow: everything pours into the inbox > you triage > sort into collections (or delete the unneeded). The inbox can be thought of as a "zero" collection - the starting point from which finds spread out into thematic groups.

## Practical scenario

```
1. All interesting traffic -> Ctrl+O -> piles up in the inbox.
2. Create collections: New -> "IDOR", New -> "Auth", New -> "Payments".
3. Sort the inbox: Move to... the corresponding collection.
4. A request important for two topics (e.g. email change in payments):
   Copy to... "Auth" and leave a copy in "Payments".
5. In the About of the "Payments" collection write: "everything about checkout and invoices".
6. Closed the topic -> right-click the collection -> Delete
   -> if there are messages, choose: return to inbox or delete.
```

## Tip

Do not breed collections uncontrollably - keep them **few and meaningful** (by bug type or by major module). And remember the **shared filter**: it acts on the inbox and on collections the same way, so if a collection seems empty - first check whether an active filter is cutting off its content (**Show all** in the filter bar), and only then go looking for messages somewhere else.
